<zone>

Zone: Recommendation Pipeline (`recommendation-pipeline`)
Files: 19, Cohesion: 0.48, Coupling: 0.52
Risk: healthy (score: 0.52)
Description: Handles the full lifecycle of LLM-generated proposals: extracting structured suggestions from AI output, acknowledging them, reconciling with existing PRD state, and converting accepted recommendations into PRD items.
Entry points: src/analyze/acknowledge.ts, src/analyze/extract.ts, src/analyze/reconcile.ts, src/analyze/scanners.ts, src/cli/commands/recommend.ts, src/recommend/create-from-recommendations.ts
Lines: 9440

<findings>

[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.52) — 22 imports target "prd-analysis-engine"
[suggestion] [info] Zone "recommendation-pipeline" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [warning] recommendation-pipeline has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio in the package (0.48/0.52) — both metrics simultaneously at their worst. This zone provides no isolation benefit while adding routing overhead for every change. Flag as fragile and prioritize absorption into prd-analysis-engine with a clear subdirectory boundary before any adjacent refactor touches shared types.
[suggestion] [info] src/analyze/acknowledge.ts and src/analyze/scanners.ts have names describing analysis concerns but are classified in recommendation-pipeline — rename or relocate to eliminate the semantic mismatch that misleads zone boundary interpretation.

</findings>

<insights>

- High coupling (0.52) — 22 imports target "prd-analysis-engine"
- The moderate coupling (0.52) is driven by this zone's 22 outbound imports into unit-analyze — recommendations are tightly coupled to the PRD schema and analysis types, which is architecturally appropriate.
- The recommend/types.ts file should be the single contract point between this pipeline and consumers; verify it is the only file imported by external zones rather than internal service files.
- conflict-detection.ts and create-from-recommendations.ts are the core business logic files; ensure they have direct unit tests since errors here silently corrupt the PRD tree.
- The cohesion of 0.48 is borderline — the mix of CLI command (recommend.ts), service logic, and type definitions suggests this zone spans two concerns: proposal ingestion and recommendation-to-PRD conversion.
- scanners.ts and extract.ts are listed as entry points, indicating multiple callers reach into the internals of this pipeline rather than going through a single facade.
- Zone "recommendation-pipeline" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- The 22:12 import ratio (outbound to prd-analysis-engine vs inbound from it) makes this the most directionally clean major dependency pair in the graph — it is the closest approximation to a proper layered interface in the codebase.
- The 4 incoming call-graph edges suggest recommendation-pipeline is consumed by very few callers despite being a distinct pipeline — if only cli commands trigger it, the pipeline boundary is effectively well-guarded even without a formal facade.
- recommendation-pipeline → prd-analysis-engine is the healthiest cross-zone dependency in the package: 22 outbound, 12 inbound, nearly unidirectional. Treat this pair as the reference model for how other zones should relate to prd-analysis-engine.
- At cohesion 0.48 the recommendation-pipeline zone sits below the minimum viable isolation threshold — it provides no encapsulation benefit over simply placing its files in prd-analysis-engine. The moderate cohesion is a signal that the community detector found weak internal bonding, not that the zone represents a genuine architectural boundary.
- reconcile.ts by name is the merge/conflict-resolution step between incoming proposals and existing PRD state; if it imports from both recommendation-pipeline and prd-analysis-engine, it is the natural extraction point for the shared interface that would break the bidirectional coupling between the two zones.
- cohesion of 0.48 is below the viable zone threshold. At this level the zone boundary adds conceptual overhead without providing isolation. The pipeline should either be absorbed into prd-analysis-engine with a clear subdirectory boundary, or its types.ts promoted as a strict one-way contract and its cohesion raised above 0.6 by removing misclassified files.
- recommendation-pipeline is the only zone that simultaneously has the lowest import-cohesion (0.48) AND the highest coupling (0.52) among all non-data zones — this exact profile (high coupling + low cohesion) is the textbook definition of a fragile zone: changes from any direction propagate through a module that provides no isolation in return.
- src/analyze/acknowledge.ts and src/analyze/scanners.ts carry names describing analysis operations (scanning, acknowledgment) rather than recommendation operations — their classification into recommendation-pipeline by import-proximity suggests they were pulled across the zone boundary by shared type imports, not by functional role. Their names are semantic mismatches with their assigned zone.
- There is no direct import path from recommendation-pipeline to cli-interface-layer in the cross-zone table — all recommendation output reaches the CLI only after passing through prd-analysis-engine. This hidden mediation means recommendation result formatting is embedded in the analysis engine rather than being a first-class CLI concern, making it invisible to CLI-layer refactors.
- recommendation-pipeline has the worst cohesion/coupling ratio in the package (0.48/0.52) — both metrics simultaneously at their worst. This zone provides no isolation benefit while adding routing overhead for every change. Flag as fragile and prioritize absorption into prd-analysis-engine with a clear subdirectory boundary before any adjacent refactor touches shared types.
- src/analyze/acknowledge.ts and src/analyze/scanners.ts have names describing analysis concerns but are classified in recommendation-pipeline — rename or relocate to eliminate the semantic mismatch that misleads zone boundary interpretation.
- [call graph] 663 internal calls, 72 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 0.9, coupling: 0.1)

</insights>
